She has shown advocacy by advancing the process (Vienna workshop, scoping discussions, business case initiative) but has not demonstrated access to the Economic Buyer or Decision Maker, has not introduced Zycus to senior stakeholders (CFO, CPO, CIO), and has not shared internal docs or defended Zycus against a competitor (no competitor named, competitive landscape unknown). identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed and well-articulated by Raiffeisen across fragmented vendor management, manual email-based quoting, and renewal workload delays. Monika Bayer owns the pain (Head of Procurement Operations) and is driving the evaluation. Specific pain points include: (1) vendor management is fragmented with no central system, leading to data silos and low visibility; (2) quoting is manual and email-driven, slowing cycle time and creating compliance risk; (3) renewal tracking is ad-hoc, causing missed deadlines and workload spikes. The 15 June Vienna workshop confirmed the pain is operational (process inefficiency, manual overhead) and strategic (RBI group strategy alignment, need for scalable multi-customer pricing). The pain is real and owned, but not yet quantified (no cost-of-delay, savings target, or ROI). The Next_Step__c (22 Jun) notes this is 'not a standard project', signaling complex/non-standard pain that may require deeper discovery and tailored positioning. The move is to co-author a quantified pain statement with Monika (cost of manual quoting, renewal delays, compliance exposure) and tie it to a compelling event (e.g., RBI group rollout timeline, contract renewal deadline).

paper_process.narrative: Paper process is in early motion. NDA was issued by Monika (8 June), Zycus provided a signed NDA (22 June), and as of 29 June Raiffeisen's countersignature is pending (7+ days elapsed, no timeline). No MSA, SOW, legal redline, InfoSec review, or procurement channel (direct vs. through partner/SI) has been discussed. The 15 June Vienna workshop was positioned as a scoping session, not a contracting kickoff. Integration touchpoints (ServiceNow, Oracle, Sirion) were raised but no formal integration scoping, IT approval, or security review has been scheduled. Until the NDA is executed, deeper commercial and scoping work may be blocked or slowed. The move is to chase the NDA countersignature with a specific due date, confirm the procurement channel (direct or partner-led), and once the NDA is signed, schedule the InfoSec / IT integration scoping session to keep the paper process moving in parallel with the business case.

decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria are implicit, derived from Raiffeisen's stated use cases and the 15 June Vienna workshop scope, but no formal RFP, RFI, BRD, or written evaluation rubric has been shared. Raiffeisen confirmed interest in Sourcing and Contract modules (150 users: 100 occasional, 50 power), ServiceNow / Oracle / Sirion integration, multi-customer pricing scalability, renewal process workflow, and contract lifecycle tracking. The Next_Step__c (22 Jun) flags that this is 'not a standard project' and requires continued scoping work, signaling that the criteria are still being refined. No formal shortlist, no competitor benchmark, and no decision scorecard have been mentioned. If Raiffeisen later runs a formal RFP or brings in a second vendor, Zycus has no locked criteria and a late competitor can redefine the rubric. The move is to formalize the decision criteria in writing (co-author a BRD or evaluation scorecard with Monika), lock the must-haves (integration, renewal workflow, multi-customer pricing), and anchor them to Raiffeisen's non-standard requirements where Zycus has differentiation (e.g., Merlin AI Intake for complex intake/approval flows).

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Raiffeisen's stated use cases (Sourcing, Contract modules, ServiceNow/Oracle/Sirion integration, multi-customer pricing, renewal workflow) align well with Zycus iSource, eProcurement, eInvoicing, and Merlin Intake product scope. However, the 22 June Next_Step__c flags this as 'not a standard project' requiring continued scoping work, signaling fit gaps or complex requirements not yet addressed. Integration touchpoints (ServiceNow, Oracle, Sirion) and multi-customer pricing scalability were raised but not validated in depth. Merlin AI Intake is in scope but under-positioned (no AI/agentic narrative surfaced in calls). Fit is partial; deeper discovery and tailored positioning are needed.

Map and engage Economic Buyer and decision maker (Arno, and any C-level final approver such as CEO referenced 22 May) to secure executive sponsorship and validate the approval chain before the business case decision. Currently single-threaded through Monika Bayer (Evaluator, Head of Procurement Ops) with no confirmed EB or DM engagement. Risk: if Monika cannot drive the decision or a competitor escalates above her, Zycus is exposed. Leverage: Vienna workshop (15 June) was positive and delivered process fit; use that momentum to request an EB briefing on the business case and ROI, positioning it as a joint leadership alignment session. This is the highest-leverage immediate move to de-risk the deal.

Co-author the quantified business case with Monika to embed Zycus ROI narrative and control the value story presented to leadership (Arno). Monika requested pricing 8 June and is building the case alone (source: Next_Step__c 22 Jun); its quantified savings/cost-of-delay content is unknown. Pain is confirmed (fragmented vendor mgmt costing manual effort, renewal delays due to resource constraints, intake chaos) but not quantified. Risk: a weak or generic ROI case lowers leadership conviction, and if the evaluation broadens to competitors, Zycus loses control of the value narrative. Recommended approach: offer a joint ROI workshop (Zycus + Monika + finance/procurement leadership) to model savings (e.g., manual quoting/email reduction, renewal cycle-time compression, compliance/vendor-risk mitigation via unified Intake+Renewal process), then co-author the efficiency concept for the CEO (referenced 22 May as a two-week deliverable, now overdue). This builds champion strength, locks Zycus into the business case, and creates a wedge against do-nothing or late competitors.

Preemptively lock scope and decision criteria with a formal joint BRD or requirements validation doc before any competitor can define the rubric. Competitive landscape is UNKNOWN (no named rival in any source, may be sole-source OR at risk of broadening during business case/leadership review). Deal is flagged 'not a standard project' requiring continued scoping work (source: Next_Step__c 22 Jun), and criteria are implicit from workshop discussions (Intake/Renewal priority, ServiceNow/Oracle/Sirion integration, operational efficiency, RBI group alignment) but not formalized. Risk: if Raiffeisen later runs a formal RFP or brings in a second vendor for comparison, a competitor can redefine the criteria away from Zycus strengths (e.g., unified Intake+Renewal, Merlin AI agentic negotiation on supplier price history). Recommended approach: position the BRD co-creation as the natural next step post-Vienna workshop, framing it as 'documenting what we validated together on 15 June so we can scope implementation and finalize pricing' — this locks Zycus as the incumbent-partner and makes it harder for a competitor to wedge in. Bonus: surface Merlin AI Intake differentiation in the BRD (Products include Merlin Intake but no AI/agentic narrative in calls yet) as a unique fit for Raiffeisen's non-standard/complex requirements and RBI group AI strategy alignment.

value.flag: Co-author the quantified business case with Monika to embed Zycus ROI narrative and control the value story presented to leadership (Arno). Monika requested pricing 8 June and is building the case alone (source: Next_Step__c 22 Jun); its quantified savings/cost-of-delay content is unknown. Pain is confirmed (fragmented vendor mgmt costing manual effort, renewal delays due to resource constraints, intake chaos) but not quantified. Risk: a weak or generic ROI case lowers leadership conviction, and if the evaluation broadens to competitors, Zycus loses control of the value narrative. Recommended approach: offer a joint ROI workshop (Zycus + Monika + finance/procurement leadership) to model savings (e.g., manual quoting/email reduction, renewal cycle-time compression, compliance/vendor-risk mitigation via unified Intake+Renewal process), then co-author the efficiency concept for the CEO (referenced 22 May as a two-week deliverable, now overdue). This builds champion strength, locks Zycus into the business case, and creates a wedge against do-nothing or late competitors.

value.flag: Preemptively lock scope and decision criteria with a formal joint BRD or requirements validation doc before any competitor can define the rubric. Competitive landscape is UNKNOWN (no named rival in any source, may be sole-source OR at risk of broadening during business case/leadership review). Deal is flagged 'not a standard project' requiring continued scoping work (source: Next_Step__c 22 Jun), and criteria are implicit from workshop discussions (Intake/Renewal priority, ServiceNow/Oracle/Sirion integration, operational efficiency, RBI group alignment) but not formalized. Risk: if Raiffeisen later runs a formal RFP or brings in a second vendor for comparison, a competitor can redefine the criteria away from Zycus strengths (e.g., unified Intake+Renewal, Merlin AI agentic negotiation on supplier price history). Recommended approach: position the BRD co-creation as the natural next step post-Vienna workshop, framing it as 'documenting what we validated together on 15 June so we can scope implementation and finalize pricing' — this locks Zycus as the incumbent-partner and makes it harder for a competitor to wedge in. Bonus: surface Merlin AI Intake differentiation in the BRD (Products include Merlin Intake but no AI/agentic narrative in calls yet) as a unique fit for Raiffeisen's non-standard/complex requirements and RBI group AI strategy alignment.
